title: Chemiluminescence of coelenterazine catalyzed by cyclodextrins as a luminescence reference standard for luminometers
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"9bpub","text":"Bioluminescence and chemiluminescence are widely used in sensitive detection methods in biomedical sciences and analytical chemistry. A limitation of this type of measurements is that luminometers and platereaders do not directly quantify absolute quantum output of the reaction but report \"relative luminescence units\" (RLU) which are specific for a given instrument and reaction vessel design. At the same time, there are no simple and convenient luminescence reference standards that would have been universally available, so results (RLU measurements) reported by different instruments and laboratories usually cannot be directly compared.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"clpgf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bpvtb","text":"I have found that cyclodextrins - which are often used to solubilize coelenterazine (CTZ) analogs and other compounds in water buffers - catalyze a weak chemiluminescence of CTZ (and its analogs). Chemiluminescence of 20 µM CTZ in the presence of 10 mM β-cyclodextrin or 10 mM trimethyl-β-cyclodextrin in the 50 mM Na-phosphate buffer (pH 7.40) can be used as a simple and convenient reference standard to define and compare RLU readings obtained by different instruments. This system is composed of only small molecules of a defined chemical composition which are not expensive and available in high purity from multiple suppliers making this system convenient for the general use as a luminescence reference standard.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"78cop","text":"CHEMICAL BASIS OF THE METHOD","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":28}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"rxb4w","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"7u8i7","text":"The method relies on the ability of cyclodextrins to catalyze light emission from coelenterazine (CTZ) in water buffers (Fig. 1). Coelenterazine itself is a high-energy compound which has the ability to emit chemiluminescence but it is very low under normal conditions (Fig. 2) and hard to reliably detect above the background on many instruments. This uncatalyzed luminescence appears to result mainly from oxidation by reactive oxygen species and to a less extent by molecular oxygen (Lucas \u0026 Solano, 1992; Teranishi \u0026 Shimomura, 1997a; Teranishi, 2006). In the presence of cyclodextrins, light emission can be enhanced to various degrees, up to more than 1000-fold (see Note 1). This is weak compared to catalysis by CTZ-dependent luciferases but sufficient for reliable detection on most instruments.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":673,"length":6}],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":487,"length":20},{"key":2,"offset":509,"length":28},{"key":3,"offset":539,"length":15}],"data":[]},{"key":"ccvik","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"89vvn","text":"In the current protocol this phenomenon was used to make a convenient reference standard for luminescence to calibrate values obtained on different instruments. This standard uses 10 mM TMCD or 10 mM β-CD (the first one leads to about 20-fold higher luminescence than the second one) to produce chemiluminescence from 20 μM CTZ in 50 mM Na-phosphate buffer (pH 7.40) (Fig. 3). All the components of these mixtures are small molecules of a defined composition. They are available from multiple suppliers in high purity and are easy to use and store. CTZ is a substrate of many widely used luciferases, so it is routinely present in many labs using bioluminescent assays. Therefore, the described assay mix could be easily reproduced giving a similar light output to use a reference. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5mn3o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"48uia","text":"This addresses the common limitation of luminescence measurements which report relative luminescence units (RLU) specific for a given instrument. There are a few possible reference standards based on chemiluminescence of luminol or on radioactivity (Lee \u0026 O'Kane, 2000) but they did not received a wide adoption due to being technically challenging. Since there are no convenient reference standards, results (RLU values) obtained on different instruments and reported by different research groups usually cannot be directly compared. For example, when measuring specific activity of purified luciferase enzymes or light emission from luciferase reporters in live cell cultures, it is usually not possible to determine how the measured absolute values compare to the values reported in previously published studies or made previously on another instrument.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":4,"offset":250,"length":18}],"data":[]},{"key":"3to6a","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"12j9d","text":"A practical example is values of weak chemiluminescence of CTZ analogs in PBS buffer in the presence of 0.5 mg/ml bovine serum albumin (BSA). This is a less repeatable reference system: up to 2-3 fold difference can be allowed between experiments conducted in different locations due to different lots of BSA and uncertainties in CTZ analog concentrations. This system leads to about 80 RLU/sec (10µM furimazine) and 170 RLU/sec (10 µM coelenterazine-h) when measured on the Biotek HTX plate reader (Biotek) used in the current protocol (default settings: PMT gain 135; BSA is cat #A-420-10 from GoldBio) while the values reported in the literature (see Fig. S4B in Machleidt et al, 2015) for the GloMax Multi+ plate reader (Promega) are about 14000 and 30000 RLU/sec, respectively (175-fold higher).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":5,"offset":577,"length":13},{"key":6,"offset":666,"length":21}],"data":[]},{"key":"6su48","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"31nty","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":7,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"42as0","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4sahr","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"vx0hr","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":8,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"44msh","text":"How the signal varies depending on differences (i.e. uncertainties and errors) in the composition of the assay mix:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":115}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5r0nf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5r0kb","text":"10 mM TMCD concentration is in the less than proportional interval of the \"luminescence - [TMCD]\" response curve, so small variations in TMCD (during preparation and due to possible variation of the water content) should not considerably affect the light output of the standard. For example, changing TMCD concentration by +5 mM or -5 mM from 10 mM leads to a +25% and -40% change in chemiluminescence (with 20 μM CTZ).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d7qih","text":"20 μM CTZ is in the semi-saturating range of the \"luminescence - [CD]\" curve for 10 mM β-CD and at the close-to-saturation range for 10 mM TMCD. For example: with 10 mM β-CD, changing CTZ concentration by +5 μM or -5 μM from 20 μM leads to a +18% and -15% change in chemiluminescence; with 10 mM TM-β-CD this causes a +5% and -20% change in chemiluminescence.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"crpd7","text":"Changing pH by -0.20 and +0.20 units from pH 7.40 results in approximately 17% decrease or 20% increase in chemiluminescence, respectively. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3qt7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"45oni","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":9,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"b2a9e","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9h0cq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e0qd4","text":"LITERATURE BACKGROUND AND SUPPORTING DATA","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":41}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"br8ik","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3dn43","text":"Coelenterazine and related compounds are poorly soluble in water-based buffers. In the PBS buffer (pH~7.4) the thermodynamic solubility of CTZ (i.e. in equilibrium with a crystalline precipitate) is below 10 μM, but supersaturated CTZ solutions up to about 50 μM can be obtained by a rapid dilution of concentrated stocks made in organic solvents.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ad1ld","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"36fmb","text":"Cyclodextrins - cyclic polymers of glucose - are often used to increase the nominal solubility of hydrophobic small molecules. Derivatives of α-CD, β-CD and γ-CD are usually used: they have a ring composed of 6, 7 or 8 glucose units, respectively, with a size of the inner cavity increasing in this row. They form a complex with CTZ by binding it in the hydrophobic cavity, and CTZ becomes solubilized as a part of such complex. β-CD derivatives are usually the most effective for CTZ. For example, about 4 mM CTZ can be dissolved in 100 mM (14% w/v) HP-β-cyclodextrin solution (Teranishi \u0026 Shimomura, 1997b). About 12 mM CTZ and 2 mM h-CTZ can be dissolved in 40% w/v (185 mM) SBE7-β-CD (Captisol) (M. Koksharov, unpublished) which is the least toxic cyclodextrin (Luke et al, 2010) for intravenous injections and serum-free cell culture. Methylated β-CD variants (DMCD, RMCD) appear to be the most efficient solubilizers of CTZ variants (M. Koksharov, unpublished) but they are toxic to mammalian cells even at very low concentrations due to the rapid extraction of cholesterol from cellular membranes (Szente \u0026 Fenyvesi, 2017; Szente et al, 2018).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":76,"length":7},{"style":"italic","offset":714,"length":11},{"style":"italic","offset":954,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[{"key":10,"offset":579,"length":28},{"key":11,"offset":766,"length":16},{"key":12,"offset":1105,"length":23},{"key":13,"offset":1130,"length":18}],"data":[]},{"key":"dk4e6","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bha2k","text":"When testing cyclodextrins as CTZ solubilizers, I have found that solutions of cyclodextrins catalyze the chemiluminescence of CTZ to a various extent depending on the type of cyclodextrin (Fig. 4). The enhancement is weak with the unsubstituted β-CD, higher with SBE7-β-CD or HB-β-CD, and the strongest with methylated β-CD derivatives. Since cyclodextrins bind CTZ in their hydrophobic cavity, this effect is consistent with previous observations that a number of proteins binding CTZ (e.g. serum albumin) can catalyze a weak chemiluminescence reaction (Viviani \u0026 Ohmiya, 2006; Vassel et al, 2012; Inouye \u0026 Sahara-Miura, 2014; Nishihara et al, 2020). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":14,"offset":556,"length":22},{"key":15,"offset":580,"length":18},{"key":16,"offset":600,"length":27},{"key":17,"offset":629,"length":21}],"data":[]},{"key":"1ndu5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"efs7f","text":"While some cyclodextrins (e.g. β-CD, HP-β-CD, SBE7-β-CD at 10 mM) do not affect or even stabilize the CTZ in solution relative to its typical half life of 3-4 hours at pH 7.4, a few cyclodextrins (\u003e10 mM) and, particularly, serum albumin (\u003e 4 mg/ml) and serum (\u003e5-10% v/v) can considerably increase the rate of CTZ degradation. RMCD and TMCD reduce it to 2-2.5 h (Fig. 3); 4 mg/ml BSA or 10% FBS (fetal bovine serum) reduce it to 35-45 min (at room temperature), and in 100% serum (FBS) at the physiological 37°C temperature the half-life of CTZ can drop to just 8 minutes. In the latter cases the weak luminescence reflects a low quantum yield but the actual catalyzed CTZ degradation can be relatively fast which can affect cell culture and in vivo applications.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":743,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ao13e","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"788df","text":"Several relevant findings were reported for vargulin - a substrate of Vargula/Cypridina luciferase - which has the same imidazopyrazinone core as CTZ. Its chemiluminescence was also shown to be enhanced by albumin and a few other binding proteins (Kanie et al, 2020).  β-CD and its methylated derivatives increased the already relatively high chemiluminescence of MCLA (a vargulin analog), with 10 mM dimethyl-β-CD providing a 6-fold increase in luminescence (Mitani et al, 1995). Chemiluminescent oxidation of luminol by hydrogen peroxide catalyzed by metal ions could be enhanced up to 30-fold in the presence of 140 mM of several cyclodextrins (Maezte et al, 2009).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":18,"offset":248,"length":17},{"key":19,"offset":364,"length":4},{"key":20,"offset":460,"length":18},{"key":21,"offset":648,"length":18}],"data":[]},{"key":"4oppn","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d418a","text":"Commonly used cyclodextrin compounds have different types of substitutions introduced at OH groups of the glucose rings. These substitutions can be variable in terms of positions and number, so different lots and products of different manufacturers tend to vary between each other, sometimes leading to differences during applications (Schönbeck et al, 2011). Among the compounds shown in the Note 1, RMCD (methyl-β-CD, randomly methylated), HP-β-CD (hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin) and SBE7-β-CD (sulfobutylether-β-cyclodextrin) have an average degrees of substitution of 11.9, 5.6 and 6.5, respectively, per a single CD molecule. The parent β-CD compound and the fully methylated (21 methyloxy groups per molecule) TM-β-CD are small molecules of a defined chemical composition, so they are convenient to use in a reference standard due to the low lot-to-lot variation in composition as long as they are of high purity.","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":22,"offset":336,"length":21}],"data":[]},{"key":"7f8dc","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":23,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"d9rs0","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2po6g","text":"ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8ea43","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"k0a0","text":"The protocol was developed at the NeuroNex Technology Hub at Brown University (\"Bioluminescence for Optimal Brain Control and Imaging\": https://www.bioluminescencehub.org) funded by the NSF (NSF grant #1707352).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":24,"offset":136,"length":34},{"key":25,"offset":191,"length":18}],"data":[]},{"key":"76c9o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"image","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"621AE0CC341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","height":280.00000000000006,"is_video":false,"legend":"{\"blocks\":[{\"key\":\"f3k0k\",\"text\":\"Fig. 1. This instrument has a complex optical system (fiber-optic cables, filters, empty space intervals) between the sample and the photomultiplier tube (PMT) detector leading to considerable losses of light. Due to these losses (and a more red-light-sensitive PMT unit), it is likely one or two orders of magnitude less sensitive than the instruments where the PMT unit is located close to the sample. solutions","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"v8un","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"length":20,"offset":292}],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"13k3r","text":"Preparation and behavior of concentrated solutions of CTZ and its analogs in organic solvents is somewhat complicated because it depends on an interplay of solubility, solubilization and supersaturation. This is commonly encountered with many hydrophobic small molecules in drug development (Brouwers et al, 2009).","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"5rbes","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"e9dg4","text":"Propylene glycol (PG) is a recommended stock solvent for most purposes.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"c02n0","text":"Take a tube or vial with a 0.5-2.5 mg sample of CTZ provided by the supplier (usually it is sold in small quantities).","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"e8kum","text":"Centrifuge to move most of the CTZ powder to the bottom.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"22n0l","text":"Add the desired volume of PG and make a suspension. To obtain a 20 mM CTZ stock, use 59 μl PG per 0.5 mg CTZ and 295 μl PG per 2.5 mg CTZ.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"4jpmi","text":"In case of a glass vial it is convenient to transfer the CTZ powder into a 1.5 ml or 0.5 ml plastic microcentrifuge tube for further handling. Do this by adding a small volume of solvent, making a suspension and transferring into a new tube. This can be repeated 1-2 times until all the powder is transferred.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"4cnak","text":"Dissolve the suspension into a (usually, supersaturated) solution according to the guidelines below.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"31t1","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":11,"offset":56,"style":"italic"}],"key":"4cplq","text":"CTZ-related compounds available from most suppliers are crystalline phase solids and have a poor solubility and solubilization propensity in ethanol or propylene glycol making it problematic to obtain more than 2-3 mM concentrations by prolonged mixing them at room temperature. When working with crystalline CTZ compounds, heating is required (60°C for CTZ) to dissolve them at high concentrations. This way, up to 60-100 mM (supersaturated) solutions of CTZ in EtOH or PG can be obtained. This short-term heating does not lead to CTZ degradation. These kinds of solutions are supersaturated and can precipitate to lower, thermodynamic solubility values under appropriate conditions but usually remain solubilized at room temperature if the solubilization during heating was complete. ","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"f2db4","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":9,"offset":63,"style":"italic"}],"key":"gim0","text":"CTZ and some of its analogs available from Nanolight (USA) are amorphous phase solids (described as being “freeze dried” by the manufacturer) which can usually be directly dissolved into supersaturated solutions of 60-100 mM in case of CTZ or h-CTZ at room temperature (but for bisCTZ heating is still required to obtain 6-10 mM solutions in PG and in PG/EtOH). ","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"8f7up","text":"Amorphous phase solids with a similar solubilization propensity can be obtained “in-house” from crystalline solids by solubilizing them first with heating in ethanol and then evaporating it quickly under vacuum (e.g. by connecting the tube top to a vacuum line).","type":"unordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"3o13","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"length":1,"offset":47}],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"cbvkg","text":"The stock solutions should be stored at -80°C.   ","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"dmc1n","text":"Propylene glycol is better than ethanol in maintaining supersaturated solutions at -80°C without precipitation during long-term storage.","type":"unstyled"}],"entityMap":{"0":{"data":{"guid":"2CCC1D508F1D11EB9180DB77E5754DFD","url":"https://doi.org/10.1002/jps.21650"},"mutability":"","type":"link"},"1":{"data":{"error_temperature":false,"guid":"C69BA2708F3111EBA15AC31030766266","id":0,"label":"","scientificNotation":false,"temperature":"-80","unit":10},"mutability":"","type":"temperature"}}}},"29":{"type":"notes","mutability":"","data":{"blocks":[{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":63,"offset":0,"style":"bold"}],"key":"fvatb","text":"Determination of the coelenterazine concentration by absorbance","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"3ulh6","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"3gp9h","text":"CTZ is usually sold in small quantities (0.5-2.5 mg) which cannot be weighted directly and even high purity samples (\u003e99%) can still contain a variable amount of a residual solvent. Therefore, a precise determination of CTZ concentration in a stock solution is required if results are sensitive to this factor.","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"7fa5","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"3uiql","text":"Both the concentration and purity of CTZ can be easily quantified by absorbance (Fig. 5) which is an intrinsic molecular property constant in a given solvent. Absorbance can be measured in high purity ethanol or methanol. Absorbance spectra in these solvents are nearly identical. Values presented in Certificates of Analysis are usually measured in methanol. CTZ and its analogs are relatively stable in ethanol at RT (for 1-2 days) even at 10-30 μM while in methanol a noticeable degradation often occurs over 1-2 days. So, generally ethanol is more convenient to use for this purpose and it is considered to be less toxic.","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"85m0q","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":3,"offset":65,"style":"sub"}],"key":"aja93","text":"Dilute the stock solution into ethanol to obtain an absorbance (A432) of 0.05-0.2 units (5-20 μM CTZ). For 20 mM stock a 1:2000 dilution will give 10 μM CTZ.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"fho8v","text":"Put into a spectrophotometry cuvette with a 1 cm light path.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"c0vis","text":"Measure absorbance at 431-434 nm (the band maximum corresponding to the yellow color of CTZ solutions) using the solvent diluted into ethanol as a blank.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"length":29,"offset":42}],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":2,"offset":85,"style":"sup"},{"length":2,"offset":90,"style":"sup"}],"key":"4qv1i","text":"Calculate the CTZ concentration using the molar attenuation coefficient ε=10000 L⋅mol−1⋅cm−1.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"2en1","text":"Absorbance at 550 nm and 350 nm can be measured as well to assess purity.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"f4u8p","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":{},"depth":0,"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"length":1,"offset":0}],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"susfn","text":" ","type":"atomic"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":2,"offset":52,"style":"sup"},{"length":2,"offset":57,"style":"sup"},{"length":2,"offset":485,"style":"sup"},{"length":2,"offset":490,"style":"sup"}],"key":"fk0d9","text":"The literature ε values vary between 7600-9800 L⋅mol−1⋅cm−1 but for preparations of high purity it can exceed 10000 (e.g. from GoldBio: cat #CZ2.5, batch #8897.061918A; cat #C-320, batch #1789.100919A). This value is convenient to use for CTZ and most other CTZ analogs (h-CTZ, bisCTZ, furimazine, etc) with the same imidazopyrazinone core structure having the same set of conjugated double bonds which determines this yellow absorption band. A considerable variation (7600-10200 L⋅mol−1⋅cm−1) of ε values for CTZ and its analogs reported in the literature and in various Certificates of Analysis is likely explained by variable amounts of a residual solvent in the preparations which is not accounted for during purity determination by HPLC and other methods.","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"a61h2","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"length":20,"offset":245},{"key":3,"length":15,"offset":585}],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"8ab55","text":"Absorbance at 550 nm is zero for pure CTZ but increases if dehydrocoelenterazine is formed (a typical major degradation product of CTZ dissolved in various alcohols and acetone). Absorbance at 350-360 nm is lower than at 430 nm (see Fig. S3A in Eremeeva et al, 2019) but will increase if CTZ is degraded into coelenteramide: a product formed during the CTZ chemiluminescence and the major degradation product in water and in some organic solvents (e.g. DMSO, β-mercaptoethanol, etc). Formation of dehydrocoelenterazine also increases the absorbance in this range (see Fig. 5.5, 5.6 in Shimomura, 2019).","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"ccfjb","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"9qmd","text":"If you want to measure absorbance at the 210-280 nm range (generally not needed for purposes described here), then methanol could be preferable since it is readily available at a higher spectral purity for this spectral range (e.g. A~0.1 at 210 nm; 99.99% purity). only 3 solvents can be recommended for most purposes and the choice depends on a particular application. They are: absolute ethanol (EtOH), propylene glycol (PG) and the 50%/50% mix of EtOH/PG. Concentrated (\u003e2-3 mM) stock solutions are stable in them when stored at -80°C (see the Tables 3, 6 and 9 in the cited patent: Kirkland et al, 2014) and can be considered operationally stable for 1-2 days even at RT.","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"a02em","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"emema","text":"PG is generally the best for making dilutions into water buffers and for the storage of concentrated stocks at -80C without precipitation. EtOH provides the highest solubility and stability at RT for many CTZ variants. PG/EtOH could be used for injections in mice and could be used as a solvent that balances some advantages/disadvantages of PG and EtOH.","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"3p16p","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":64,"offset":824,"style":"italic"}],"key":"fn4ks","text":"EtOH provides the highest thermodynamic and supersaturation solubility of CTZ and some of its analogs. It also appears to be the most stabilizing solvent: even low (10-30 μM) concentrations are relatively stable at RT for several days, while similar CTZ solutions in methanol or isopropanol show a faster degradation at RT. The stabilization can be in part due to antioxidant impurities in the grain-derived ethanol rather than the property of the solvent itself (see below on methanol). Concentrated (supersaturated) solutions of CTZ (\u003e20 mM) may precipitate during storage at -80°C. EtOH is volatile, so it can evaporate when handling μl volumes or at the edge of the liquid/air interface leading to a solid CTZ precipitate (film). The easy evaporation can be used for a solvent-free transfer into other organic solvents, but such precipitates are very hard to dissolve in water buffers, even with cyclodextrins, once the precipitate film is formed.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"length":17,"offset":542}],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"49qit","text":"Propylene glycol is convenient since concentrated solutions generally remain soluble when stored at -80°C. Its relatively high viscosity requires slower handling but is still perfectly compatible with any micropipetting procedures. It has a lower wetting of polypropylene than EtOH or water which, together with a higher viscosity, provides an easier handling in many handling procedures involving microtubes and plastic tips (e.g. collecting by centrifugation or placing micro-liter volumes on tube walls before mixing). It is non-volatile (Võ \u0026 Morris, 2012) for practical purposes, so its micro-liter volumes won't evaporate. Low (10-30 μM) CTZ concentrations will show a noticeable degradation after 1 day at RT but millimolar stocks are relatively stable at RT for 1-2 days. Propylene glycol is compatible with osmotic pumps if needed.","type":"ordered-list-item"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"length":20,"offset":79},{"key":3,"length":23,"offset":376},{"key":4,"length":7,"offset":607},{"key":5,"length":5,"offset":634}],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":7,"offset":687,"style":"italic"},{"length":8,"offset":699,"style":"italic"}],"key":"at8n3","text":"50% EtOH / 50% PG mix was described as one of the storage solvents by Promega (Kirkland et al, 2014) and Prolume companies (USA). This mixture provides a balance between the higher solubilization ability of EtOH, the non-precipitation of supersaturated solutions in PG and a low viscosity. The Prolume (Nanolight) company originally recommended this mixture (NanoFuel-Inject, cat # 304 NF-CTZ-FB-INJ) for intravenous and intraperitoneal mouse injections of CTZ variants as a relatively well tolerated solvent compared to pure ethanol or methanol. Tannous, 2009) and later also in acidified ethanol (Charles et al, 2014). Solutions of CTZ variants in a pure methanol are often not stable during long-term storage at -20°C. Therefore, acidified methanol (with 10-60 mM HCl) was often used in which CTZ is more stable and can be stored for many months at -20°C or -80°C. Acidified methanol or ethanol is usually prepared by supplementing it with 60 mM HCl by adding 52 µl concentrated HCl (36% w/w = 12.1 M) per 10 ml of absolute alcohol. If a nearly completely water-free acidified alcohol solvent (i.e. methanolic or ethanolic hydrogen chloride) is desired, it can be prepared by adding 60 mM of acetyl chloride.","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"cn3g2","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"fsvto","text":"However, such stocks offer no apparent advantage over pure absolute ethanol, so the solvents described in the Note above should usually be used. steps: Bring the reference assay solutions (with 10 mM β-CD and/or with 10 mM TMCD) to room temperature.
Turn on the luminescence plate reader or luminometer, warm it up and make it ready for the measurements.

Take out a small (20-40 μl) aliquot of 15 mM CTZ in propylene glycol from -80 °C and allow it to reach Room temperature in the dark. The 15 mM CTZ stock of the correct concentration should give an absorbance of 0.150 at λ=431-435 nm upon a 1:1000 dilution into absolute ethanol or methanol (ε=10000 L⋅mol −1 ⋅cm −1 ).

Prepare a tube to make a desired volume of the reference luminescence solution. 1.5-2 ml tubes can be used for smaller volumes and 12 ml tubes can be used for larger volumes. For example, add 1.5 ml 10 mM cyclodextrin assay solution to a 1.5-2 ml tube or add 3 ml to a 12 ml tube.

Calculate the amount of the 15 mM CTZ stock required to obtain the 20 μM final solution in the assay mix. For example, use 2 μl of stock solution for the 1.5 ml volume and 4 μl for the 3 ml volume. The presence of 18 mM propylene glycol (obtained as a result of the 1:750 dilution of the CTZ stock made in propylene glycol into a water-based assay buffer) does not affect the chemiluminescence values when compared with a vehicle-free 20 μM CTZ solution. The latter can be obtained by evaporating the required μl volume of a CTZ stock made in ethanol and its re-dissolution in the cyclodextrin assay buffer by a thorough mechanical mixing.

Place the required small volume of the 15 mM CTZ stock above the level of the buffer as several micro-drops (on the tube walls or under the cap). 0.93 μl volumes of 15 mM CTZ stock (in propylene glycol) are placed under caps of 1.5 ml microtubes containing 700 μl buffer - for subsequent rapid mixing by vortexing.

Close the tube and mix by vigorous vortexing and shaking. Make sure that CTZ is fully solubilized. In the presence of 10 mM β-CD or TM-β-CD the 20 μM concentration of CTZ is nominally soluble and should go into solution after a rapid vortex mixing. If using a solid CTZ residue, e.g. after evaporation (rather than micro-droplets in PG or other solvent), then a thorough mechanical mixing may be required to bring it into solution even in the presence of cyclodextrins. In the absence of cyclodextrins or other solubilizers (e.g. serum) in water buffers, 10-20 μM CTZ is in the supersaturated concentration range at pH 7.40 (above the thermodynamic solubility), so some yellow crystals can occasionally be observed if mixing was not rapid enough.

Quickly dispense the final assay mix into the microplate wells or tubes which will be used for luminescence measurements. An opaque white 96-well plate with samples. rows C,D: 100 μl of 20 μM CTZ in 50 mM Na-phosphate buffer (pH 7.40) containing 10 mM TMCD (columns 1-4) or 10 mM β-CD (columns 5-8); row E: the same as in rows C,D but 200 μl; rows A-B: 5 and 0.5 μM fluorescein in 0.1 M NaOH (used as a fluorescence reference in a separate experiment);

Start to measure luminescence within the 5-10 min after mixing CTZ with the assay solution, because chemiluminescence will slowly decay. 96-well plate in the loading tray of the plate reader. A single-tube luminometer. Chemiluminescence signal can be averaged for the first 10-20 min for TMCD and 30 min for β-CD. The chemiluminescence signal slowly decays (as CTZ is slowly oxidized) but can be considered stable for practical purposes during the first 10-20 min with 10 mM TMCD and 30-50 min for 10 mM β-CD (see the Figure below). Time profiles of luminescence decay during the first 30 min for 20 μM CTZ dissolved in the presence of 10 mM TMCD (A) or 10 mM β-CD (B) in 50 mM Na-phosphate buffer (pH 7.40). The recording was performed on Synergy HTX platereader (Biotek, USA) in a 96-well white opaque plate (Perkin Elmer, USA). The PMT detector gain was 135 (a default value). See also the Table 1. Chemiluminescence of CTZ with TMCD is about 20 times higher than with β-CD making TMCD a more convenient luminescence standard. The two reference solutions can be used to complement each other as two independent reference standards with the different light output. With not very sensitive instruments, the chemiluminescence with β-CD can be not very far from the detection limits making in some cases its use problematic. β-CD is available at low prices and from a wider number of suppliers compared to TMCD A cheaper RMCD (randomly methylated β-CD) can be used in place of TMCD for routine reference measurements, since it leads to a similar light output. However, it must be initially referenced against TMCD/β-CD because this randomly methylated compound can differ considerably lot to lot. How the signal varies depending on differences (i.e. uncertainties and errors) in the composition of the assay mix: the signal depends on CTZ concentration with a slightly less than proportional or semi-saturating response which depends on the type of cyclodextrin (see the Guidelines section) the response is less than proportional relative to the cyclodextrin concentration (see the Guidelines section) changing pH by -0.20 and +0.20 units from pH 7.40 results in approximately 17% decrease or 20% increase in chemiluminescence, respectively.

Use the measured luminescence values (usually, the relative light units per second, RLU/sec) as a reference standard to calibrate the RLU values of the instrument relative to other instruments or to variations in the values generated on the same instrument at different dates due to changes in its operation. If the same reference standard is measured on another instrument, then the RLU values produced by these instruments can be directly compared by calculating an appropriate conversion coefficient. The Table 1 below shows the reference values obtained for the Biotek HTX platereader in this work. Examples of reference values that can be obtained. Reference RLU values of the CTZ/cyclodextrin standards obtained on the Biotek HTX plate reader with different plates. The PMT gain was set to 135 (default value) in all measurements. The instrument can measure luminescence either with the bottom or the top fiberoptic probe collecting the emitted light. The probe was located at the well center (below or above). The top probe height was 3.1 mm above the top surface (of the plate plate or lid if the latter was used). *a - the background signal of the plate with an empty moat; *b - the background signal of the plate with a moat filled with 30 ml black agar gel (2% charcoal powder, 1% agar) to reduce the bioluminescence cross-talk between transparent wells; The chemiluminescence of these reference solutions can also be used to assess and reference the sensitivity of imaging instruments. Below is an example of a weak chemiluminescence of these solutions captured on Azure 600 imager (Azure Biosystems, USA). A transparent CytoOne 24-well plate contained 500 μl samples in the following wells: TMCD reference solution in A1-A3, β-CD reference solution in D4-D6, a plain buffer in C1-C3 and all other wells were left empty. The plate was imaged for 300 sec using the chemiluminescence mode with the 3x3 pixel binning. The instrument obtains 32-bit tiff images which have an intensity range of white from 0 (black) to 65535. The image in the panel "A" was saved as jpeg file without any adjustments, while in the panel "B" the intensity was trimmed to the 63742-65535 range to highlight the observed chemiluminescence. A) Unadjusted 32-bit image (300 sec exposure, 3x3 pixel binning). Range of white: 0-65535. B) The same image adjusted. Range of white: 63742-65535. Azure 600 imager with a 24-well plate inside.
